questions.question: Explain the difference between register stack and memory stack organization. What are the advantages of register stack?
keyPoints: Register stack uses CPU registers for storage vs memory stack uses RAM
Register stack provides faster access (single cycle vs memory cycles)
Limited by number of registers before spilling occurs
Memory stack has larger capacity but slower access

questions.question: What is the purpose of Stack Pointer (SP) and Frame Pointer (FP) in register stack organization?
keyPoints: SP tracks the top of the stack and current stack position
SP is updated on each PUSH and POP operation
FP provides stable reference point within stack frame
FP remains fixed during function execution for parameter/variable access

questions.question: Explain the concept of stack overflow and underflow in register stack organization.
keyPoints: Overflow: occurs when all registers in register stack are filled
Underflow: occurs when attempting POP from empty stack
Both are error conditions requiring handling
Overflow can be managed by spilling to memory

questions.question: What is register spilling? When and why does it occur?
keyPoints: Spilling moves register contents to memory when register stack is full
Occurs when number of nested function calls exceeds available registers
Extends logical stack size to physical memory
Involves write to memory (spill) and read from memory (fill) operations

questions.question: Differentiate between caller-saved and callee-saved registers with examples.
keyPoints: Caller-saved: can be overwritten by called function; caller saves if needed
Callee-saved: must be preserved by called function; restore before return
Example callee-saved: BP, SI, DI in x86; $s0-$s8 in MIPS
Example caller-saved: AX, CX, DX in x86; $t0-$t9 in MIPS

questions.question: Describe the components of a Stack Frame (Activation Record).
keyPoints: Return address - where to continue after function returns
Saved registers - callee-saved registers
Function parameters - arguments passed to function
Local variables - variables declared in function
Optional: saved FP, temporary storage

questions.question: Explain how function call and return are handled in register stack organization.
keyPoints: CALL pushes return address onto stack or link register
Prologue: push old FP, set new FP, allocate space for locals
Function executes using FP for variable access
Epilogue: restore FP, pop stack, return using return address

questions.question: Explain the complete process of function call execution in register stack organization, including prologue, execution, and epilogue phases.
keyPoints: Prologue: Save old FP, set new FP, allocate space for locals, save callee-saved registers
Execution: Access parameters via negative offsets from FP, locals via positive offsets
Parameter passing: First few parameters in registers, rest on stack
Epilogue: Place return value in designated register, restore saved registers, restore FP, pop stack frame, jump to return address
Stack growth direction, SP/FP updates at each step

questions.question: Compare register-based calling conventions with memory-based calling conventions. Discuss the trade-offs in terms of performance, stack depth, and implementation complexity.
keyPoints: Register calling: parameters in registers, faster access, limited parameters
Memory calling: all parameters on stack, slower but more flexible
Trade-off: register faster but limited depth before spilling
RISC architectures favor register-based for performance
Memory-based provides larger capacity for deep recursion
Complexity: register requires careful register allocation

questions.question: Design and explain a register stack organization for a processor with 16 general-purpose registers. Include details of register allocation, stack pointer implementation, and handling of overflow conditions.
keyPoints: Allocate registers: 4 for stack control (SP, FP, reserved), 8 for register stack, 4 for temporary/special
Register stack uses 8 registers in circular or linear manner
Overflow handling: when 8 registers full, spill oldest entries to memory
Need clear push/pop semantics and boundary checks
Include status register for overflow/underflow flags
Consider memory backup area for spill operations
